text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
Yesterday we looked at the gold of the lampstand pointing to the divine source of Christ’s light, the weight and the hammer blows pointing to the costliness and suffering that shaped Him, the base pointing to Christ as the unshakeable foundation, and the central shaft pointing to Christ as the true vine who holds every branch. Today we study what remained; the six branches, the remarkable three-stage flower on each one, the wick, the oil, the fire, and the light itself.
By the time we have finished, we will see that the lampstand is one of the most complete portraits of Christ in the entire tabernacle.

From the central shaft extended six branches; three on the left and three on the right. Together with the central shaft, they made seven lamp positions in total. Seven is the number of fullness and completion in Scripture. The lampstand with its seven lamps declared that the light of God was complete, full, and lacking nothing. Not five lamps looking for two more. Not six lamps nearly there. Seven; the complete and full light of God burning in the Holy Place.

The six branches point to the congregation that Christ holds and through which He shines. Revelation 1:20 reveals the mystery directly: “The seven lampstands are the seven churches.” The church is the lampstand. Christ is the shaft.
The branches are the assembled, called-together people of God; the Kohathites, the congregation, extending from Him in every direction and burning with the light that comes from being connected to Him. No branch produced its own light. Each branch simply held the lamp that burned with oil and fire.
In the same way, the congregation of the new covenant does not produce its own light. It holds the light of Christ and lets it burn in the world.

On each of the six branches were three cup-shaped decorations, each carved in the form of an almond flower with three parts: a bud, a blossom or petal, and a ripe almond. Three stages on each cup. Nine stages on each branch.
Six branches of nine each makes 54. The central shaft had four of these same three-stage cups, making 12. And 54 plus 12 equals 66; the exact number of books in the Bible. The entire Word of God, Old Testament and New Testament, is pictured in the cups of the lampstand. And all 66 cups are held on one lampstand, with one shaft at the centre. All 66 books of Scripture are held together in Christ. He is the theme of every book, the centre of every page. Remove Christ from Scripture and the lampstand has no shaft; it collapses.

But the three stages on each cup, bud, blossom, almond also speak of the life of Christ in three stages. The bud speaks of His birth and early life; the promise not yet fully opened, the life of God present but not yet fully revealed to the world, growing quietly in Nazareth. The blossom speaks of His public ministry the opening of the life of God before the eyes of Israel, the preaching, the healing, the teaching, the full fragrance of His character poured out in three and a half years of ministry. And the ripe almond speaks of His death and resurrection. The almond, as we have seen, is the first tree to bloom after winter the sign that death does not have the final word. The ripe fruit on the lampstand declares that Christ passed through bud and blossom and death, and came out bearing ripe, incorruptible fruit on the other side.

The lampstand burned with pure olive oil; the finest grade, beaten or pressed from olives, with nothing added and nothing inferior mixed in. The oil had to be continuously replenished. Without the oil, the flame would die. No matter how beautiful the gold, no matter how perfectly the wick was trimmed, if the oil ran out the light went out. The oil was not optional. It was the entire source of what fed the flame.

The oil speaks of the anointing that rested on Christ without measure. John 3:34 says: “He whom God has sent utters the words of God, for He gives the Spirit without measure.” Every other man who received the Spirit received it in measure; a portion, a measure suited to their calling. Christ received the Spirit without measure, the full, unrestrained, overflowing anointing of God upon Him. Isaiah 61:1 gives Christ’s own declaration: “The Spirit of Yahweh is upon me, because He has anointed me.” And Luke 4:18 records Him standing in the synagogue at Nazareth and reading those very words, saying: “Today this Scripture is fulfilled in your hearing.” The oil in the lampstand was pure and unceasing. The anointing on Christ was pure and without limit.

The flame that burned on the lampstand was not lit by the priest with an ordinary fire. Leviticus 16:12 tells us that the fire on the golden altar was taken from the bronze altar of sacrifice in the outer court & God lit this fire. The fire originated at the place of atonement and sacrifice. It was holy fire, set apart, carried from the altar into the Holy Place to light what burned before God.
No strange fire was permitted. Leviticus 10:1 records the death of Nadab and Abihu, who offered strange fire; fire not taken from the prescribed source, and were consumed. The fire had to come from the right place.

Christ is the fire and the light. John 8:12 records His direct declaration:
“I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness,
but will have the light of life.” And the fire that He brought came from the altar of sacrifice; the ransom sacrifice, from the place of atonement. The light of Christ in the world is inseparable from His sacrifice. It is not a light of good teaching alone, or wisdom alone, or example alone. It is the light of a life laid down and raised again; fire that came from the altar. Any light that is not rooted in the atonement of Christ is strange fire. Any gospel that removes the sacrifice and keeps only the teaching is strange fire. The light of the lampstand was only valid because it was lit from the right source; and the light of Christ is only saving because it comes from his ransom sacrifice.

The lampstand burned in the Holy Place so that the priests could see to serve. It illuminated the Table of Showbread on the north wall. It illuminated the Golden Altar of Incense before the veil. Without the lampstand, nothing else in the Holy Place could be seen or used. Christ is the light that makes everything else visible; the Word, the prayer, the fellowship, the presence of God.
Without Him, everything is in the dark. With Him, the whole room is lit.

title: Prayer
text:
Yahweh, You are my light and my salvation; whom shall I fear? (Psalm 27:1). You sent the true light into the world, and the darkness could not overcome it (John 1:5). We thank You for every detail of the lampstand; for the branches that remind us we are held in Christ and called to burn with His light, for the three stages of bud, blossom, and almond that show us the full story of His life, death, and resurrection written in gold, for the oil without measure that anointed Him so He could anoint us. Forgive us for the carbon we have allowed to build up the sin, the pride, the compromise that has caused our light to flicker and smoke. Take the golden scissors to us. Trim what needs to be trimmed. We trust the hand that holds them, because it is a golden hand; a divine hand that trims in love, not in anger. May we burn brightly in the Holy Place of this world.
May the light of Christ in us illuminate everything around us.
In Christ’s name, amen.
